description.description: Imagine drawing a straight line through a scattering of dots to show a trend. It predicts an outcome based on a single factor (like predicting ice cream sales based purely on the temperature outside).
The starting point of our line. For our ice cream example, it's how much ice cream you'd sell if the temperature was absolute zero.
The slope of our line. It tells us how much the outcome changes when our factor goes up by one (e.g., selling 10 more ice creams for every 1 degree hotter).
Like Simple Linear Regression, but using multiple factors at once. For example, predicting a house's price based on its size, number of bedrooms, AND age, rather than just size alone.
The baseline starting value before we consider any of our factors.
The weights of importance assigned to each factor. It tells us exactly how much a specific factor (like adding an extra bedroom) impacts the final outcome.
Despite the name, this is used for sorting things into buckets rather than predicting numbers! It calculates the odds of a Yes/No outcome, like categorizing an email as 'Spam' or 'Not Spam'.
The baseline odds of an event happening before any evidence is gathered.
How much each piece of evidence tips the scales towards a Yes or a No.
Like asking your closest neighbors for advice. If we want to classify a mystery fruit and its 5 most statistically similar neighbors are all apples, the algorithm guesses it's an apple too.
How many neighbors to ask! A small K might be swayed by a weird outlier neighbor, but a massive K might just blend everyone together.
The math formula used to decide who your 'closest' neighbors actually are.

An unsupervised sorting hat! If you dump a massive pile of unlabelled data on it, K-Means will naturally organize it into distinct groups by finding similarities, like sorting a messy room into categorized boxes.
The exact number of boxes you want to sort the data into. You have to tell the algorithm how many groups to look for.
How the algorithm chooses its initial starting points to begin grouping the data.
A flowchart that makes decisions by asking a series of Yes/No questions. 'Is it raining? Yes -> take umbrella. No -> Is it sunny? Yes -> wear sunglasses.'
The mathematical rule the tree uses to decide what the best Yes/No question to ask is at any given step.
How many questions deep the flowchart is allowed to go. Stopping it early prevents it from overcomplicating things.
A democracy of Decision Trees. Instead of relying on one single flowchart (which might be biased), you build 100 slightly different flowcharts and let them all vote on the final answer.
The size of the voting committee. More trees usually means a better, more stable prediction, but it takes longer to calculate.
How deep/complex each individual tree in the forest is allowed to grow.
Imagine trying to draw the widest possible street between two different neighborhoods of data points. SVM tries to separate categories with as much "buffer room" as possible.
A mathematical trick that allows SVM to draw curvy or 3D boundaries if a straight line isn't enough to separate the groups.
A strictness dial. A high 'C' means the algorithm will bend over backwards to avoid misclassifying a single dot, while a low 'C' prefers a smoother, softer boundary.

A lightning-fast, probability-based guesser. It's "naive" because it assumes every piece of evidence is completely independent from one another (like assuming having the word 'Win' and having 'Cash' in an email are unrelated coincidences when aggressive spam filtering).
Telling the math whether it's looking at bell curves of continuous numbers, or distinct categories.
An algorithm that identifies dense crowds of data points and ignores the isolated loners (noise). It's amazing for finding oddly shaped clusters, like predicting constellations in the sky.
The maximum distance two points can be apart for them to be considered 'in the same crowd'.
The minimum number of people required to officially constitute a 'crowd'.
A virtual brain made of interconnected layers of 'neurons'. Starting with raw input, each layer passes signals forward and practices turning raw data into an intelligent guess.
The 'spark' inside a neuron that decides whether the neuron should fire or stay dormant based on the signals it just received.
How deep the brain is. Adding more layers allows the brain to understand more complicated, abstract concepts.

description.parameter: Number of Neurons per Hidden Layer
description.description: The width of the brain. Adding more neurons in a layer allows the network to memorize more intricate patterns.

A specialized brain designed specifically for looking at imagery. It scans images using small mathematical magnifying glasses to piece together shapes, textures, and eventually complex objects like faces or cars.
The magnifying glasses (filters) scanning the image to look for specific visual patterns like horizontal lines, corners, or colors.

parameters.description: How many different types of patterns the layer is searching for simultaneously.
The physical size of the magnifying glass sliding over the image.
How many pixels the magnifying glass shifts over with every step. Larger strides shrink the image faster.

description.description: A simplification step. It deliberately compresses the image down by keeping only the most important recognized features, reducing the computational load.

parameters.description: The size of the window used to summarize and compress the image section.

description.description: Takes the 2D visual maps and squashes them into a single 1D line of data so a regular brain (Dense Layer) can analyze it.
A standard interconnected neural network layer that acts as the final decision-maker, taking all the visual clues and making the final guess.
Randomly turns off a percentage of neurons during training to prevent the brain from over-memorizing the exact training images.

An extremely powerful, competitively tuned team of decision trees. It builds trees sequentially, where each new tree specifically tries to fix the mistakes made by all the previous ones. It's a favorite algorithm for winning Kaggle data science competitions!
Like a student learning from their mistakes. It builds a model, sees exactly where it failed to predict correctly, and builds another model focused purely on correcting those specific failures.
Controls how much of a contribution each new tree makes. Smaller learning rates are slower but usually lead to better accuracy.

Teaching the computer to read text and determine the underlying emotional tone—whether the writer and text is happy, angry, or neutral. Great for automatically reading product reviews or monitoring social media pulses.
A model designed to sort free-form text into predefined categories, like instantly organizing incoming news articles into 'Sports', 'Politics', or 'Technology' bins.
